title: Week 1 Challenge — Build Your Virtual Share Portfolio
objectives: Apply share buying mechanics to a virtual portfolio
Calculate total invested cost including brokerage
Evaluate three NZ companies using basic share data

vocabulary.definition: A simulated investment portfolio using real share prices but no real money. Used for learning and practice without financial risk.

questions.question: Which statement BEST describes what you own when you hold an ordinary share in Mainfreight?
options: A debt instrument — Mainfreight owes you money at a fixed interest rate
A proportional ownership stake in Mainfreight — entitled to voting rights, dividends if declared, and a share of residual assets if wound up
A guaranteed return equal to the NZX 50 index performance
A certificate that Mainfreight will repurchase at a predetermined price in 5 years
questions.explanation: Owning an ordinary share means owning a fraction of the company — not a loan or guaranteed return instrument. Your rights include voting at AGMs, receiving dividends if the board declares them, and participating in residual assets if the company is wound up. But there is no guaranteed return, no obligation for Mainfreight to buy shares back, and your dividend is not guaranteed.

questions.question: An IPO prospectus shows the company will use 70% of IPO proceeds to repay loans from its original private equity owners. What should this signal to an ordinary investor?
options: The company is financially responsible — reducing debt is a positive sign
Caution — most of the money raised goes to insiders exiting their investment, not to growing the business; ordinary investors are effectively buying out the private equity investors
This is legally prohibited in NZ — all IPO proceeds must go to the company's operations
The higher the debt repaid, the better the investment — less debt means a stronger company post-IPO
questions.explanation: When a large portion of IPO proceeds goes to repaying existing investor loans rather than funding growth, it signals that insiders are using the IPO primarily to cash out. New investors are essentially buying the original investors' exit. This is not necessarily a dealbreaker, but it means the company is not raising the capital for growth — there is less fuel for the business going forward, and the people who know the company best (original investors) are choosing to sell.

questions.question: You place a limit buy order for 500 Fisher and Paykel Healthcare shares at $21.00. The current market price is $21.85. What happens?
options: Your order executes immediately at $21.85 — limit orders always execute at the best available price
Your order is rejected — limit orders must be within 1% of the current market price
Your order sits on the exchange and will only execute if FPH shares fall to $21.00 or below — it may never execute
You receive 500 shares immediately at $21.00 and the $0.85 difference is credited to your account
questions.explanation: A limit buy order only executes at your specified price or lower. Your $21.00 limit sits on the order book waiting for a seller willing to accept $21.00 or less. If FPH never falls to $21.00, the order never executes. You can cancel a limit order at any time. Limit orders are useful when you want to buy but feel the current price is too high — you set your price and wait.

questions.question: You buy $2,000 of Spark shares on Sharesies (0.5% brokerage). Spark pays a $0.135 dividend per share. If you own 476 shares (at $4.20 each), what annual dividend do you receive?
options: $6.43 — 0.5% of $2,000 minus brokerage
$135.00 — the dividend per $1,000 invested
$270.00 — 0.135% of the $2,000 investment
questions.explanation: 476 shares × $0.135 dividend per share = $64.26 annual dividend income. This represents a dividend yield of $64.26 ÷ $2,000 = 3.21% on the original investment. Spark typically pays this in two instalments — approximately $32 each in March and September. This is passive income you receive simply for holding the shares.

questions.question: Which NZ platform charges brokerage as a percentage of transaction value (with a cap) rather than a flat fee per trade?
options: Jarden Direct — $30 flat fee makes it best for all trade sizes
Sharesies — 0.5% on NZX trades (minimum $0.50, capped at $25) making it best for smaller amounts
Hatch — specialises in US shares only at flat USD $3 per trade
InvestNow — direct share trading with percentage brokerage on all NZX transactions
questions.explanation: Sharesies charges 0.5% on NZX trades with a minimum of $0.50 and a cap of $25. This makes it the most cost-effective for smaller trades — on a $100 trade, Sharesies charges $0.50 versus Jarden's $30. Hatch is USD-denominated for US shares only. InvestNow is an index/managed fund platform, not a direct share trading platform.

activities.title: Build Your Virtual NZX Portfolio
activities.setup:
You have $10,000 virtual dollars to invest in NZ shares using Sharesies (0.5% brokerage, capped at $25). You must invest in at least 3 different NZ companies.

Current share prices (use these):
Fisher and Paykel Healthcare (FPH): $21.40
Spark NZ (SPK): $4.18
Mainfreight (MFT): $67.80
Meridian Energy (MEL): $5.54
Infratil (IFT): $11.20
Air New Zealand (AIR): $0.72
Contact Energy (CEN): $8.35

Dividend information:
FPH: $0.38/year
SPK: $0.275/year
MFT: $1.50/year
MEL: $0.20/year
IFT: $0.165/year
AIR: $0.00 (no dividend)
CEN: $0.38/year
activities.challenge:
1. Choose 3 to 5 companies and decide how many shares to buy in each. Keep track of all allocations.
2. Calculate the brokerage cost for each purchase.
3. Calculate your total invested (shares + brokerage) and confirm it does not exceed $10,000.
4. Calculate the annual dividend income from your portfolio.
5. Calculate the dividend yield on your total portfolio value.
6. Write a one-paragraph investment thesis for each company you chose — why did you pick it? What risks concern you?
hints: Q2: Brokerage = 0.5% of trade value, minimum $0.50, maximum $25.
Q4: Annual dividends = shares held × annual dividend per share, summed across all holdings.
Q5: Portfolio yield = total annual dividends ÷ total invested × 100.
activities.solution:
Sample portfolio (one of many valid combinations):

1. Allocations:
FPH: 200 shares × $21.40 = $4,280. Brokerage: $4,280 × 0.5% = $21.40
SPK: 800 shares × $4.18 = $3,344. Brokerage: $3,344 × 0.5% = $16.72
MEL: 400 shares × $5.54 = $2,216. Brokerage: $2,216 × 0.5% = $11.08

2. Brokerage: FPH $21.40, SPK $16.72, MEL $11.08. Total brokerage: $49.20

3. Total invested: $4,280 + $3,344 + $2,216 + $49.20 = $9,889.20. Under $10,000 ✓

4. Annual dividends:
FPH: 200 × $0.38 = $76.00
SPK: 800 × $0.275 = $220.00
MEL: 400 × $0.20 = $80.00
Total: $376.00

5. Portfolio yield: $376 ÷ $9,889 × 100 = 3.80%

6. Investment thesis examples:
FPH: World-class NZ medical device company with global distribution and strong R&D pipeline. Risk: currency exposure (most revenue in USD) and high P/E valuation.
SPK: Stable NZ telco with high dividend yield. Risk: regulatory environment and competitive pressure from 2degrees.
MEL: NZ electricity generator — renewable energy (hydro, wind, geothermal). Benefits from electricity demand growth and climate transition. Risk: dry hydro year reduces generation.

activities.title: Why Did You Choose What You Chose?
activities.prompt: Looking at your virtual portfolio: Did you choose companies whose products you use personally? Did you choose the highest dividend yield? Did you choose companies you thought would grow? Write a short reflection on what drove your choices — and whether those reasons are good investment rationale or just familiarity bias.
activities.nceaLink: Extra Learning — analysing your own decision-making process is one of the most valuable investment skills.
